data.title: Sensient Technologies: The Bull Case Looks Priced In
data.description:
Thesis

It’s been almost three years since I last wrote about Sensient Technologies Corporation (SXT), a company behind the products you barely notice but absolutely experience: the colors in your drinks, the flavors in your snacks, the coatings on your pills, and the pigments in your cosmetics. Basically, Sensient sells the sensory layer.

Back then, I had a hold rating because I liked the company's long-term prospects; however, in the short term, there were several headwinds preventing me from being overly bullish toward SXT at that time, including destocking of inventory, volume weakness across multiple end user groups, and margin pressure on the company's profit. In my view, the potential for upside from the company did not outweigh my concern regarding these headwinds.

Initially, after the call, SXT had a rough go, trading down to some extent, but then it slowly built back and consolidated for a while. From there, almost a year ago, just before its Q1 2025 EPS and revenue beats, shares broke out significantly, eventually reaching all-time highs.

That said, after digging into Sensient’s latest Q1 2026 results and taking another look at my rating, I’m still staying neutral. I think that the natural color opportunity is clearly interesting. But, at the same time, the stock already seems to reflect a lot of that upside. And because expectations look pretty full, the margin for mistakes is extremely small.

Is This Growth Story Bigger Than It Looks?

  • Q1 2026 revenue: $435.83M
  • Revenue growth: 11.09% Y/Y
  • Local currency revenue growth: 7%
  • Color Group local currency revenue growth: 12.3%
  • Natural color conversion invoiced (last 9 months): ~$20M
  • Prior conversion invoiced (back half 2025): ~$5M

The revenue increase was across all product lines, with the Color Group being the standout; that unit has been accelerating because of both continued sales from the base business and other factors, such as the U.S. market gradually switching from synthetic to natural colors. This acceleration was two-pronged; growth in the base business continues to be achieved through product differentiation based on technical superiority. The Color Group’s growth cannot be attributed to volume pre-loads (product accumulated in customer inventory in advance of new orders), but rather to new wins added to the pipeline; new wins are driving most of the growth in the Color Group during the quarter. Pricing made a cameo, but it wasn’t exactly the main act. Most of the growth came the old-fashioned way: selling more stuff and selling better stuff.

  • Color Group adjusted EBITDA margin: 24.4%
  • Color Group local currency operating profit growth: 13.2%
  • Local currency adjusted EBITDA growth: 10%

Meanwhile, margins held up better than feared. Heading into the year, Sensient Technologies warned investors that profit margins in Color would probably dip because they were spending a ton of money building out capacity before the revenue from that capacity actually showed up. Instead, margins stayed flat. Why? Because the new customers they won were on the trickier, more complex end of color conversions, and those jobs pay better. This bodes well for future profit margins for Sensient Technologies. Management was clear: margins will trend flat for at least the first half of the year, and material improvement won’t become apparent until the fourth quarter of the calendar year. They are incurring expenses now and anticipating that revenue will ramp up shortly thereafter.

  • Natural color sales goal: $1B
  • Synthetic color revenue base: ~$100M
  • Conversion ratio: ~10:1

The fact that regulatory deadlines are real and companies have a vested interest in meeting them is beyond doubt. The current spike in natural colors is not just hype; there are hard deadlines forcing companies' hands. For example, Walmart (WMT) has committed to reformulating its private-label products by January 2027 to remove artificial food colors (FD&C). Synthetic food color products will no longer be permitted for sale in West Virginia beginning in January 2028. The state of Texas will require warning labels on all products containing artificial FD&C colorings as of January 2027. These changes represent shelf consequences. As such, customers are continuing to develop reformulated versions of their current products, despite worries about how the current economic climate may affect their ability to spend.

The process of switching from artificial to natural colors (for just one product) typically requires a time frame of six to twelve months. A supplier must not only substitute one ingredient for another, but also ensure the color remains stable, has the appropriate appearance, does not change the product’s existing flavor profile, and conforms to regulatory requirements. As suppliers, Sensient Technologies and some of its competitors, including GNT Group, Oterra, and DDW, assist their customers not only by selling the appropriate color but also by providing technical help and support throughout the reformulation process. Therefore, once a customer begins working with a manufacturer’s technical staff on a particular project, it becomes very time-consuming and financially expensive for that customer to switch to another supplier mid-process. My point in bringing this up is that this creates a true advantage for Sensient Technologies over its competitors. The worry: as the easier, more straightforward conversions get done, competition will get fiercer on the remaining harder ones. So it’s worth watching whether Sensient Technologies can hold onto that edge.

  • Flavors & Extracts local currency revenue growth: 1.7%
  • Flavors & Extracts local currency operating profit growth: 5.1%
  • Flavors & Extracts adjusted EBITDA margin: 17.2%
  • Asia Pacific local currency revenue growth: 4.7%
  • Asia Pacific local currency operating profit growth: 14.5%
  • Asia Pacific adjusted EBITDA margin: 26.1%

The Flavors and Asia Pacific divisions were also successful; each surpassed expectations, with profit levels growing at a faster rate than revenue levels in each division, demonstrating operational discipline. In Asia Pacific, management did caution that continued improvement will depend, in part, on the Iran conflict being resolved because of its impact on energy costs, shipping costs, and certain raw material costs. As you know, that’s completely out of their hands, and it’s a genuine wild card in this equation.

  • Q1 2026 cash flow used in operations: -$14M
  • Q1 2026 capex: $29M
  • 2026 capex guidance: $150M–$170M
  • Natural color capex plan (next few years): $225M–$250M
  • Net debt / credit adjusted EBITDA: 2.4x
  • Q1 2026 interest expense: $7.9M
  • Q1 2025 interest expense: $7.3M
  • Q2 2026 expected interest expense: ~$9M
  • 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance: high-single to double-digit growth
  • 2026 adjusted EPS guidance: high-single to double-digit growth

In the meantime, the company had negative cash flow this quarter, which was anticipated because it's a seasonal occurrence; moreover, Sensient Technologies has purposely built up inventory for the anticipated surge of natural color orders from its customers. Sensient's debt (as measured by a leverage ratio) is currently in the low twos; however, management believes the ratio will rise as the company continues to spend on factories and build up inventory. Management also feels comfortable with such an increase in debt because they still believe that the revenue will be forthcoming; even so, if their customers’ reformulation timelines slip, that financial cushion may diminish. Another point here is that management boosted their full-year profit (EBITDA) projection by a larger amount than they raised their EPS projection. If this may seem unusual, it's not. The reason is that, over the course of the year, borrowing costs (interest on all that debt) continue to increase and reduce the bottom line. One analyst from BNP Paribas caught this and flagged it; management confirmed it openly.

  • 2026 revenue guidance: high-single to double-digit local currency growth
  • Natural color sales goal: $1B
  • Natural color conversion invoiced (to date): ~$20M

Essentially, Sensient Technologies believes that the changeover from artificial to natural food colors will happen at a much more rapid pace than its current forecasts are predicting. As a result, they're building additional factories, sourcing necessary raw materials, and hiring employees accordingly. If they are correct, then they will look like geniuses for being so well prepared for the changeover process; however, if their customers take too long to put this strategy into effect, then they could have a stockpile of costly excess inventory and capacity. Right now, the early evidence suggests the bet is going reasonably well.

SXT's Valuation

As I write this, pre-market, it looks like SXT shares are getting an uppercut to the chin. It most likely has to do with the Iranian "wildcard" I just mentioned. Specifically, Brent Crude (Jun ’26) was trading at $126. That's not really good for most of us, nor SXT. As you know, this war has swung back and forth between tentative peace and renewed fighting. My read has been that the experts predicting a longer, potentially wider regional conflict have, so far, had the better odds than those of us who thought it might be resolved sooner.

Even before today's trading, on the raw numbers, SXT wasn’t cheap. At $120.54, FAST Graphs has it trading at a blended P/E of 33.33x, well above its normal 19.74x multiple and more than double the 15x fair value line. Meanwhile, that implied fair value comes in significantly lower, around $77.83, telling me that the market's already pricing in a substantial amount of success from the natural color conversion cycle.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s valuation table, it basically indicates the same thing in a different language: forward non-GAAP P/E is 31.33x versus the sector median of 16.05x, while forward EV/EBITDA is 17.63x versus 8.23x for the sector.

Part of the premium is justified by the growth profile. Forward revenue growth of 8.20%, forward EBITDA growth of 14.40%, and forward EPS growth of 16.47% are all above sector levels.

The longer-term EPS growth estimate of 17.50% also supports a higher multiple. My concern is that the stock price has risen faster than the business has turned profits into cash.

Cash flow is still negative, capex is rising, debt is likely to increase, and inventory is building ahead of customer conversions.

Final Takeaway

Hold. I’d stay patient here rather than chase it. SXT does have a real growth driver with the shift to natural colors. But, at this point, the stock already seems to be pricing in a good amount of that upside. It is also trading at a premium multiple, so the risk/reward no longer looks clearly attractive.

So, if I already owned it and believed conversions could pick up into 2027, I’d be fine holding. But for new money, I’d wait for a better entry point. And if I cared a lot about valuation, I definitely wouldn’t chase it here.

data.title: Varonis Systems: Potential For Topline Growth To Accelerate
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Varonis Systems (VRNS) a few years ago, which I recommended a buy rating as I expected the SaaS transition to drive growth acceleration, which should also help to drive a positive multiple re-rating, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. In my view, the transition has now played out well. The question is no longer whether VRNS can get through the model change. It mostly has. What matters now is whether the cleaner SaaS model, better new customer momentum, and broader AI-related demand can support the next leg of growth. I believe it can, and hence, I stay buy-rated.

Earnings review

VRNS started FY2026 strong. Total revenue was $173.1 million, up 27% y/y from $136.4 million in 1Q25. The mix kept moving toward SaaS. In the quarter, SaaS revenue was $161.1 million, up from $88.6 million vs. last year. Meanwhile, term license subscription revenue fell to $6.9 million from $31.5 million, while maintenance and services revenue fell to $5.2 million from $16.4 million. While adj. gross margin is down slightly from 80.2% last year to 77.9% this quarter, adj. EBIT margin actually went up, from -4.7% last year to -0.8%, which effectively means VRNS should turn profitable next year. The key takeaway here is that revenue growth accelerated, SaaS became a bigger share of the business, and non-GAAP profitability improved, even if gross margin compressed.

Post-transition growth is becoming more apparent

Fast forward almost 2.5 years since my last update, and the SaaS transition has clearly played out well. SaaS ARR excluding conversions grew 29% y/y to $522.6 million, which I think is the cleanest number in the quarter because it strips out the noise from customers moving from the old model to the new one. More importantly, the quarter also showed why the underlying business now looks better after the transition. Specifically, management said sales reps are spending less time on conversion work and more time on new customers, upsells, and selling more of the platform. We see this showing up in the Q1 results, where management said stronger new customer contribution was the main driver of the quarter’s outperformance. That is possibly one of the most important takeaways in the quarter. Recall that during the transition, one of the biggest drags was sales capacity, as they needed to allocate resources for migration vs. finding new customers. And that slowdown in new customer contribution may have led to some investors thinking demand has weakened. Now that the migration is done, the sales reps have more time to go after new customers, which should drive the next leg of growth.

There is another important takeaway here. Management said that long-term growth should lean more on the existing SaaS base, with the majority of annual contract value [ACV] expected to come from current customers. This is a huge statement because management is basically saying the actual value of the existing customer base is not reflected in the P&L today, and new customers acquired today will be worth a lot more in the coming years. And since we know more customers are going to be acquired (now that sales reps are focused on that), the customer lifetime value of the VRNS customer base should continue to see durable growth ahead.

AI is widening the platform

Now that the SaaS transition is done, the equity story now leans more on AI and data security. The basic idea is that customers are prioritizing data and AI security because guardrails are needed before they connect more of their data to AI systems. For those unaware, data security control is a real problem when it comes to adopting AI. Companies want the productivity benefit, but they do not want models and agents pulling sensitive data they should not touch.

This is where VRNS comes into play, and it is working out nicely. In this recent Q1, VRNS continued to see adoption of Managed Data Detection and Response [MDDR] and AI-related products, and also products that help to secure cloud environments. We also know that VRNS is winning large deals, as management pointed out a large new customer win from a global technology company with >50,000 employees, and that customer used VRNS across various environments (AWS, Salesforce, Google Cloud Platform, Google Drive, MDDR, Copilot, etc.). Even a large, prominent name like ServiceNow is also expanding its use of VRNS to cover internal AI systems and email security. This tells us that the VRNS platform is being used across multiple workloads and use cases. In simple terms, the product works and can work at scale.

This demand dynamic also changes the way I view VRNS’s newer product strategy. We know that customers are using VRNS, and if VRNS can roll out better products to address AI concerns, we should see healthy demand here. So, when management highlighted Database Activity Monitoring, Interceptor, and Atlas as seeing early traction, I thought that was very encouraging, as it suggests VRNS has a lever it can pull to accelerate growth.

Valuation

I remain bullish on VRNS. Looking at the growth momentum and potential, I raise my revenue growth assumptions, now expecting revenue growth to accelerate to 25% by FY2028, with FY2026 achieving the midpoint of management guidance (implying 18% y/y growth). Valuation-wise, the average revenue growth expectation is ~18% over the past 10 years, and the average forward revenue multiple over the same period is 7x. I am staying conservative here by assuming just 6x while growth is above the historical average. That brings me to a share price target of ~$61.

Risk

The growth narrative lies on better new customer momentum, broader platform adoption, and early interest in newer products like Atlas. If growth does not accelerate despite these, it may imply weak spots of demand somewhere in the business. This would weigh on the growth acceleration narrative that I expect to play out.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on VRNS. I believe the equity story is getting better. The SaaS transition is no longer an overhang; the cleanest growth metric accelerated; and demand is starting to broaden across more products and use cases. If topline growth accelerates as I expect, the stock should see a nice positive re-rate.

data.title: Constellium SE: Q1 Earnings Beat, Buy On Operational Growth
data.description:
Constellium SE (CSTM) is a global aluminum processing company that designs and manufactures high-value, engineered aluminum products that are used primarily in aerospace, automotive, and packaging applications. They don’t make their revenue from the raw aluminum itself, but rather from the value added by rolling, extruding, and engineering the metal into specialized components. The stock has seen tremendous growth in the last year, up 240%. The company just reported Q1 earnings with major wins and an optimistic outlook from management, with the stock up another 5% immediately on the news.

CSTM benefits from long-term structural growth in aluminum demand as manufacturers shift toward lighter and more energy-efficient materials. Earnings growth, cash flow guidance, and discounted valuation are green lights for this stock, and I have CSTM as a buy. The price of aluminum can cause them volatility, but their improvements in EBITDA per ton leave me less worried about the metal's pricing swings.

Q1 Snapshot

CSTM just reported a record adjusted EPS of $1.47 for Q1 2026, smoking expectations and up from $0.26 a year ago. That is now back-to-back quarters that earnings absolutely crushed analyst projections. Revenue was $2.5 billion, up 24% YoY, even though shipments were actually down 1% total.

Adjusted EBITDA has moved from $186 million last year in Q1 to $359 million this quarter, as each of their 3 segments saw nice advancements, with their largest segment, Packaging & Automotive Rolled Product, seeing a 152% increase. This gain does include a metal price lag positive impact of $97 million. Management alluded to favorable metal costs and lower operating costs as key drivers.

They repurchased 1.2 million shares for the quarter worth $28 million, bolstering investor confidence, and saw net income arrive at more than 400% higher than prior Q1. There is more to dissect, but the major fundamentals showcase a huge win for CSTM in Q1 results.

Guidance, Cash Flow & Leverage

CSTM CEO Ingrid Joerg noted that as a majorly global company, macroeconomic and geopolitical fronts have created some challenges, but the firm has remained persistent and even is raising guidance on the remainder of 2026. They now expect adjusted EBITDA in the range of $900-$940 million, not including metal lag effects, and also anticipate a huge ramp-up in free cash flow to reach more than $275 million. Free cash flow is rising on the price of aluminum rising significantly in the last year, as well as operational efficiencies improving the cash they make per ton sold. $275 million would be much higher than they have experienced in the past and would put them in a great position to weather any storm that comes with the cyclical nature of metal prices. The excess cash will also help in their deleveraging process that is now down to 2.2x, with hopes of reaching a target range as low as 1.5x.

Seeking Alpha analysts have EPS and revenue revisions all higher for CSTM, with a full-year earnings outlook of $2.09. I think this number is going to get smashed by year-end and will come in closer to $3.00, if not greater.

Aluminum Risk

No question the price return in the last year for CSTM can be at least partially correlated to the rapid rise in aluminum prices since about exactly a year ago. This is a risk for CSTM, as inevitably, aluminum prices will fall again. Personally, I don’t think that the metal is going to have a sharp drop anytime soon like it did in 2022 on recession fears and weakening demand. Revenue dropped for CSTM in 2023-2024 as the metal’s price was subdued, and this does need to be noted. However, there are two reasons that leave me less concerned about the volatility of the commodity for CSTM. Global aluminum demand looks quite strong for the next several years due to EV automotive, aircraft, and solar expansion. Number two, CSTM has done a fantastic job on enhancing operations, and has seen a generous improvement in cash from operations YoY and EBITDA per ton in each of their core segments.

Outpacing the Aluminum Risk

With aluminum prices fluctuating, it's important for CSTM to find another avenue toward sales or margin expansion. In many earnings calls, CSTM refers to “conversion margin,” essentially as their way of profiting from processing aluminum, not from owning the actual metal. There is not a specific ratio to measure this, but they do track EBITDA per ton shipped, which is the best available proxy to understand how efficient they are at producing and shipping aluminum.

CSTM saw adjusted EBITDA per ton increase by 6% in their Aerospace & Transportation segment, 54% in their Automotive Structure segment, and 159% in their Packing & Automotive Rolled Product segment. This is a fantastic sign that even when aluminum prices swing, they are positioning themselves to grow internally and not be dependent on the commodity itself.

The rising EBITDA per ton is telling us that they are seeing a better product mix with higher margin aluminum going to the aerospace and electric-vehicle industries in particular. These operational efficiency gains are demonstrating how the firm is moving more toward growing earnings per ton, not just tons shipped, and is trying to wean away from being just a commodity processor.

Solid Value

CSTM is trading at a discount from all angles. The stock has more than doubled in price since last fall and has managed to drop its PE from 87x to now 16x in that span. A remarkable run and position from a valuation standpoint. EPS over the last four quarters is $3.14, up from $0.46 in the prior 4 quarters. Their price-to-book is slightly elevated over the sector median, but price-to-sales and PEG are majorly discounted to the median. I think a PE closer to 30x is probably fair for CSTM. That would be a little above the industry, which I think is fair considering the growth they have had. I think the stock has very strong fundamentals and that investors have not yet fully priced in the company’s growth success.

Conclusion

CSTM just reported a huge beat in earnings along with strong guidance commentary from management in direction for the remainder of 2026. The stock is certainly reaping the benefits of the currently elevated price of aluminum. This is their biggest risk, but who’s to say that the metal isn’t going to remain high, considering demand across the globe is steady? Even with that risk, the company has done a fantastic job in improving on their “conversion margin,” with great advancement in their EBITDA per ton shipped. This is their ultimate pathway to growth in this industry and in becoming less reliant on metal pricing. Management expects their free cash flow to expand to record highs this year, and the stock is trading at a big discount at the moment. Buy CSTM.
